The topic of self-driving cars is highly debated especially in light of recent event with the death of an Arizona woman at the hands of a self-driving Uber. Those for self-driving cars argue that they reduce the rate of accidents as a result of distracted drivers, improve traffic flow, are cost saving in terms of time and monetary value from insurance, and could allow greater access to transportation for the disabled and elderly. Those against self-driving cars argue the system does not eliminate accidents, that there is a learning curve with the technology, that it would take away profit from other sectors of the economy, and that there are instances in which the technology is not equipped to make a decision. While both sides make valid points, the benefits of adopting a system of self-driving cars rises to the forefront. 

Many people are quick to point out the flaws in self-driving cars but it is still in its infancy and has the ability to develop and improve with time whereas we have not seen that in human motorists over the years. Certainly we may expect the number of accidents by self-driving cars to rise as it is more widely adopted but this number palls in comparison to the number of accidents caused by drivers. Tragedies such as those in Arizona should not happen but unfortunately with nearly 1.3 million people dying in motor vehicle accidents a year and 81 percent of accidents being the result of human error it appears the main culprit is us. As humans we already rely on computers and technology for many daily tasks and self-driving cars are an extension of the innovative mindset our tech fields have embraced. 

Those against also argue that investing in self-driving cars may take away money from other industries and sectors of the economy. Again while self-driving cars may impact the profits of other transportation system, overall the profits from developing and implementing this technology will outweigh the losses. There is a reason many people invest in tech companies as the have become more profitable in the 21st century. Especially with the start-up of new technology, as they industry grows this will demand that more and more jobs be created to replace the ones lost. Also, savings in terms of reduced accidents are astronomical. The U.S. Department of Transportation has assigned a value of 9.2 million for each human life, with savings coming from venues such as healthcare and insurance. There is also the argument that self-driving cars may also save you time as they free up your transportation time to attend to other daily tasks when you do not have to focus on the driving. With more efficient driving the self-driving cars have the potential to decrease driving time and traffic as well.

Though people are still resistant to the idea of self-driving cars it is already in ways being implemented into our driving with features such as autonomous braking, self-parking and sensor detectors. The technology for this field is currently available and calls on our investment to develop and improve. While no system is perfect, one that saves us lives and on costs should be highly considered.

output_text:
The debate over self-driving cars intensified after the widely reported death of an Arizona pedestrian struck by a self-driving Uber vehicle. Supporters of autonomous vehicles argue they will reduce accidents caused by distracted or impaired drivers, improve traffic flow, save time and money through reduced insurance and healthcare costs, and expand mobility for elderly and disabled individuals. Critics counter that autonomous systems will not eliminate accidents, that a learning curve and edge cases will persist, that the technology may disrupt jobs and industries, and that machines sometimes cannot make ethically clear decisions. While these concerns are important, the potential benefits of widespread adoption of self-driving cars are compelling.

Autonomous vehicle technology is still maturing, and its flaws are often highlighted—rightly so—but development and iterative improvement are inherent to technological progress. Human driving behavior, by contrast, has not shown comparable improvement in safety over time. Although the introduction of self-driving cars may initially reveal new types of incidents, the scale of human-caused accidents dwarfs these early failures. Globally, motor vehicle collisions claim roughly 1.3 million lives annually, and studies attribute about 81 percent of crashes to human error. Given that humans already rely on computers for many daily tasks, extending automation to driving is a logical next step in reducing preventable harm.

Economic objections deserve consideration but do not outweigh the net gains. While autonomous vehicles could disrupt sectors tied to traditional driving, growth in technology sectors historically generates new industries and jobs that offset losses. Investment in self-driving technology is expected to spur employment in software, hardware, maintenance, data services, and infrastructure. Moreover, the cost savings from fewer accidents are substantial: the U.S. Department of Transportation assigns an approximate value of $9.2 million per statistical life, and reduced collisions would lower expenditures across healthcare, insurance, and emergency services. Self-driving cars also promise personal time savings by allowing occupants to use commute time productively, and more efficient driving could reduce congestion and travel time overall.

Adoption is already underway in incremental forms: features such as automatic emergency braking, lane-centering, adaptive cruise control, self-parking, and parking sensors are common on modern vehicles. These assistive technologies demonstrate both the feasibility and immediate benefits of automation while providing a foundation for further development. No system is perfect, and autonomous vehicles will require careful testing, regulation, and ethical frameworks to handle complex scenarios. Still, given their potential to save lives, reduce costs, and increase mobility, continued investment and deployment of self-driving technology merit serious consideration.

Self-driving car is a new technology and receives a lot of debates recently. On one hand, self-driving cars offer positive impacts, e.g. safer commuting, cost savings, and give opportunities to many people who can't travel by themselves at this moment. On the other hand, people also raise concerns about the security, safety, and economy of the technology.
In my opinion, I support bringing self-driving cars into reality. The technology can bring many benefits to humanity. First, we, as human in modern societies, will save a lot of money thanks to the technology. Because when a lot of self-driving cars are on the street and they can communicate with each other, they can reduce the problem with congestion or unknown traffic conditions. Second, self-driving cars are supposed to be more safety because it can eliminate mistakes made by a human. The most common mistake is driving under the influence (of alcohol, drugs, or tireness). Moreover, drivers tend to have false confidence as they have more driving hours. This false confidence will increase the chances that an accident will happen. Last but not least, self-driving cars put an equity for people having difficulties in traveling, such as old people or disabilities. While public transportations are available in all cities, in many cases, they can only take a person to a pre-designated location (stop) rather than the real destination. Public transportations are operated with a fixed schedule which does not perfectly match everyone's situations. 
The cons about self-driving cars could be categorized into two groups: not-ready and not-true. The not-ready group contains arguments about new challenges that the technology will bring in when it is deployed in mass. For example, when an accident is made by a self-driving car, who will hold the responsibility? Or security issues such as how can drivers protect their personal data and what will happen if the car is hijacked by a hi-tech criminal? Interestingly, what would the car-makers or software designers choose in moral-sensitive situations? For example, given an unavoidable accident will happen, which will either kill an old pedestrian crossing the street or a baby in a stroller on the pavement, which choice should be made by the self-driving car? These issues must be addressed or disclosed before deploying self-driving cars in the mass. The not-true group consists of false assumptions about the self-driving technology. For example, there are concerns about drivers are no longer equipped with the skills to operate cars. In fact, we are living well in a society without knowing much about our daily stuffs. I don't know anything about fixing a car, but I can always call AAA or my friends whenever my car breaking down. I use the washing machine at least twice a week without knowing how to make such a machine. Another false opinion is the difference between states regarding turning right on red or cars cannot interpret police officers' signals when directing traffics. In fact, the car can update its algorithm according to the state it is traveling simply based on GPS signal. Moreover, when all cars on the streets are self-driving cars, the police officer does not need to go down the street and directs the traffic but just sends information to the car through a communication channel, e.g. 3G or wifi. Last but not least, some people even afraid that auto manufacturers might sell fewer cars. This is not true as a self-driving car is still a car which needs a manufacturer. In fact, we have seen more and more car manufacturers join the self-driving race nowadays, such as Volvo, BMW, Toyota, Honda, and many more.
In conclusion, I think self-driving car is a revolution that will have more benefit to our society. However, there are still some concerns needed to be addressed in order to make this technology available to everyone.

output_text:
Self-driving cars are an emerging technology that has sparked intense debate. Proponents point to many potential benefits—safer travel, cost savings, and increased mobility for people who currently cannot drive. Critics raise valid concerns about security, safety, and economic impacts. I support bringing self-driving cars into everyday use, provided that the outstanding challenges are responsibly addressed.

The technology promises substantial advantages for modern societies. First, widespread adoption of autonomous vehicles could reduce congestion and improve traffic flow. When cars communicate with each other and with infrastructure, they can optimize routes, maintain smoother speeds, and react faster than human drivers to changing conditions, saving time and fuel. That efficiency translates into significant cost savings for individuals and for society as a whole.

Second, self-driving cars have the potential to improve road safety. Many crashes result from human error—driving under the influence of alcohol or drugs, driving while tired, distraction, and overconfidence from accumulated driving hours. Autonomous systems do not get distracted or impaired, and they can maintain consistent attention to the road. While no technology can entirely eliminate accidents, automation can reduce the most common human errors that lead to fatalities and injuries.

Third, autonomous vehicles can increase mobility and equity for groups who have difficulty driving today: older adults, people with disabilities, and those unable to access convenient public transit. Public transportation is crucial but often limited by fixed routes, stops, and schedules that do not match every individual’s needs. Self-driving cars could provide door-to-door service on demand, creating new independence for many users.

Concerns about self-driving cars fall into two categories: legitimate readiness issues and misconceptions. Readiness issues require careful attention before mass deployment. Legal and ethical questions remain: who is responsible when an autonomous vehicle crashes—the manufacturer, software developer, owner, or operator? How will we protect users’ data and guard against sophisticated cyberattacks or vehicle hijacking? Ethical dilemmas—such as how a vehicle should act in an unavoidable harm scenario—must be debated openly and addressed through regulation and transparent design.

Other worries are based on false assumptions. Some fear that drivers will lose the ability to operate vehicles at all, but modern society already relies on specialists and services for many technical tasks; routine users need not be mechanics to benefit from a technology. Concerns about regional traffic rules or interpreting police signals are also overstated: vehicles can update software based on GPS and receive real-time instructions from authorities via secure communication channels. Finally, fears that manufacturers will sell fewer cars ignore that self-driving vehicles are still cars; manufacturers are actively entering this market, not leaving it.

In conclusion, self-driving cars represent a transformative technology with substantial social and economic benefits. To realize those benefits safely and fairly, we must address legal, security, and ethical challenges through regulation, robust engineering, and public dialogue. With those safeguards in place, autonomous vehicles can improve mobility, safety, and efficiency for everyone.

Self-driving cars, though only recently becoming a technological feasibility, have advanced rapidly in their short time of development and have thoroughly captured the public eye. The debate on their fitness to our automotive environment and on their desirableness to our society as a whole is raging onward with impassioned argument on both sides. In this essay I will aim to provide convincing evidence that though it is not perfect, the prospect of driver-less roads would mark a definitive improvement over our current conditions. 

To start with, consider the fallibility of human drivers. On a regular basis we turn on our engines without sufficient sleep, absentmindedly forget to check blind-spots, and allow our attention to be torn from the road by the slightest of mobile vibrations. With an estimated 33,000 annual fatal accidents and overwhelming evidence that human error accounts for a significant number of these it seems incontrovertible that we should do better. Although it is not yet certain that self-driving cars in their current condition would cause that number to go down, the lack of human fallibility makes the prospect seem likely. Furthermore, whereas human driver tend to fall into a lull of false confidence when they have engaged in a task for an extended period of time, self-driving cars can be designed to improve and become more safe over time. There is no distraction, no sleep deprivation, no intoxication, and no frantic concern for being late to take a self-driving car away from the task of safely getting from point A to point B. 

Furthermore, self-driving cars could give society a method of transportation which is much less restrictive. Currently, driving privileges are limited to those capable of operating the machinery, passing their test, and -for the most part - affording to own their own vehicle. If a child needs to be transported to any sort of extracurricular event they are bound by their parents' schedules and mobilities. If a senior citizen who has been deemed unsafe to drive decides to go shopping they must wait for long periods to use public transportation with conditions that could be extremely painful or uncomfortable with those who experience chronic pain from arthritis or similar conditions. Furthermore, many who cannot afford their own car are forced to commute long distances to their jobs, health-care, and programs, forcing a de facto restriction on who may access what. Although it is likely that one who cannot afford a regular car could not afford their own self-driving car, there is nothing to say that they must have their own. The ability to have a car drive back to the place it left from could allow a much more intricate form of car sharing; additionally, the current restrictions on insured drivers that hassle any type of shared vehicle would likely be thoroughly relaxed or missing entirely for a driver-less car. Even beyond the prospect of shared cars, self-driving vehicles could revolutionize public transportation, making a wider range of pick up locations and drop off locations feasible. If large buses or a huge fleet of public cars were to have flexible routes that could give people who do not have the means to own their own car a transportational freedom akin to it at a fraction of the price that services like Uber and Lift offer today. 

A society which supports self-driving cars would certainly look different from ours. How we implement them will determine much of their success and failure. For that reason it is not a guaranteed success, however, looking at the massive limitations and problems with our current system, it does not seem to be a stretch to claim that this could bring us dramatic improvements.

Self-driving cars, though only recently having become a technological feasibility, have advanced rapidly in their short time of development, managing to thoroughly capture the public eye. A debate on their fitness to our automotive environment and on their desirableness to our society as a whole has been raging onward with impassioned arguments on both sides. In this essay I aim to provide convincing evidence that, though they have potential to cause societal harm, given the distressing array of negative societal impacts associated with human-driven cars, the apparent feasibility for self-driving cars to ameliorate these issues makes it only rational to welcome their implementation. 
	To start with, consider the fallibility of human drivers. On a regular basis we turn on our engines without sufficient sleep, absentmindedly forget to check blind-spots, and allow our attention to be torn from the road by the slightest of mobile vibrations. With an estimated 33,000 annual fatal accidents and overwhelming evidence that human error accounts for a significant number of these, it seems incontrovertible that we should do better. 
	While no one would rightly argue that our current system is ideal, skepticism toward self-driving cars at this stage is more than fair. The development of the vehicles is very much still in its developmental infancy. Coupling that fact with the reality of a mixed automotive environment - with both computerized and human driven cars - one might very well expect implementation of self-driving cars to cause an increase in accidents. The key question then becomes: "would a self-driving car actually be any safer than a human-driven one?". While there is currently insufficient information to give a definite answer, I think there are good reasons to put more faith in the self-driving cars. Whereas human drivers tend to fall into a lull of false confidence after driving for many years, self-driving cars can be designed to improve and become more safe over time. Further, there is no distraction, no sleep deprivation, no intoxication, and no frantic concern for being late to take a self-driving car away from the task of safely getting from point A to point B. In the absence of such risks, a computerized car might very well behave safer even without the ability to intuitively grasp tendencies and perceptions of neighboring human drivers. 
	Others however, are more concerned about the societal impact of these cars than their safety specs. Even if the change means less lives lost on the road, it still may be an unwise one if it leads to the ruin of lives off the road. Given the number of transportation jobs, a computerized revolution could displace the occupations of many currently employed drivers. Furthermore, given the massive economic investment needed to own such a car, might this lead to a widening of already egregious inequality gaps in the country? 
	The transportation industry represents a real threat if we have no place for the newly unemployed to turn. This however, is not a problem unique to self-driving cars but rather, is a pervasive and imminent threat encroaching on us from all directions given the rapid advance of technological competency. If we cannot, as a society solve this more generally, the threats will be realized even in the absence of the driver-less car revolution; if we do find a way to support the displaced workers of other sectors I remain hopeful that such approaches can be implemented to the transportation-sector as well. 
	As for societal inequality, self-driving vehicles, if implemented properly, could actually be a great boon to economically struggling classes. To begin, the previously considered transportation industry automation, though bad for workers, could be fantastic for commodity prices. If the cost of all daily goods dropped, many would be the better for it. Further, current systems of public transportation are woefully insufficient to serve their desired societal role. When jobs, hospitals, and enrichment events are inconvenient to access, attendance from under serviced individuals tends to drop. This creates a de-facto restriction on who may access what. With an automated public transportation system, it could be much easier to introduce flexible pick-up locations, drop-off spots, and schedules. With a more functional and convenient public transportation system, we could relieve both the economic burden of buying a vehicle as well as the logistical burden of navigating tricky and time-consuming bus routes. 
	A society which supports self-driving cars would certainly look different from ours. How we implement them will determine whether those differences were successes or failures. For that reason it is not a guaranteed success, however, looking at the massive limitations and problems with our current system, it is reasonable to maintain optimism about the ability of self-driving cars to bring about dramatic improvements to our lives.

output_text:
Self-driving cars, once a distant sci-fi notion, have quickly become a practical reality and captured widespread attention. Debate over whether autonomous vehicles belong on our roads and what role they should play in society is fierce. In this essay I will argue that, while not flawless, the widespread adoption of driverless cars promises a meaningful improvement over our current transportation system.

Begin with human fallibility. Routinely people drive tired, neglect blind spots, and have their attention stolen by a buzzing phone. With roughly 33,000 fatal crashes per year in the U.S. alone and strong evidence that a large share stem from human error, it is clear our present reliance on human drivers has serious costs. Self-driving vehicles remove many of the most common causes of crashes: they do not suffer from fatigue, distraction, alcohol impairment, or the overconfidence that can set in during long drives. Although today’s autonomous systems are not perfect and will require further refinement, their potential to reduce accidents is plausible because they can be engineered to follow rules consistently, react faster in many scenarios, and improve through software updates and machine learning.

Beyond safety, autonomous cars could transform accessibility and mobility. Currently, driving privileges depend on physical ability, licensing, and the means to own a vehicle. Children rely on caregivers’ schedules, and older adults who can no longer drive often face long waits for transportation or endure uncomfortable journeys that aggravate chronic pain. People who cannot afford a car are constrained in where they can work, seek healthcare, or participate in community life. Self-driving vehicles could change this dynamic. They enable new models of shared mobility—vehicles that can drop a passenger off and return to a central hub or pick up the next rider—reducing the need for individual ownership. Insurance and regulatory frameworks would need to adapt, but driverless operation could relax some of the current restrictions that complicate car sharing. Moreover, autonomous vehicles could enhance public transit: fleets of smaller on-demand vehicles or flexible bus routes could deliver more frequent, direct service to a wider range of pickup and drop-off points, offering car-like convenience at much lower per-user cost than today’s ride-hailing services.

Adopting self-driving cars would reshape communities and everyday life. How we design regulations, infrastructure, and business models will determine whether the transition yields net benefits. There are legitimate concerns—technical failures, cybersecurity, job displacement in driving professions, and ethical decisions in rare crash scenarios—that must be addressed. Still, weighed against the persistent limitations and harms of our current system, the potential improvements in safety, accessibility, and efficiency make a compelling case that driverless roads could be a substantial step forward.

draft1:
There are many advantages for the United States to transition to self-driving cars. The primary benefit is simply the amount of lives saved due to the elimination of intoxicated, distracted, or otherwise dangerous drivers. Computers provide a precise, safe alternative to the potential for human error that occurs each time a driver gets behind the wheel. In addition, self-driving cars can save money on insurance costs and outdated infrastructure, decrease traffic, and are actually better for the environment.  However, some limitations to self-driving cars exist. These include a difficult transition to using these vehicles, cost, the failure of technology, and the potential for job loss and decline of certain industries.
	Frequently, we hear arguments for or against something with the justification that it benefits or does not benefit society.  In order to make an argument for either one of these, it is imperative that we consider the following questions: who is society, what is good for society, what does society need, and at what expense to the individual do we support the public good?
	In this case, the answer to these questions is easy. Anything that has the potential to save human lives is both good for the individual and good for society. Despite the limitations in technology, an initial cost, and a likely challenging transition, self-driving cars are the future, and they will save lives. No other benefit matters. No other limitation should stop us.  
America has a serious, dangerous driving problem. Every year, 33,000 people die in automobile accidents, and most of these deaths should have been prevented because the drivers were distracted, drunk, tired, or simply should not have been driving.  
If that statistic is not sobering enough, consider this. Vehicular accidents are also the number one cause of death for teenagers, again, the majority of which are preventable.  We know from cognitive scientists and psychologists that teenagers are more likely to engage in risky behavior—like dangerous, drunk, or otherwise impaired driving—and, while getting a driver’s license has been a rite-of-passage for a century in America, it’s time to stop the senseless destruction car accidents cause.
Interestingly enough, the Department of Transportation calculated that each person is worth $9.2 million. Like so many things, the government got this one wrong as well. A human life is priceless. Nothing can bring back a loved one who has been killed.
If you have reservations about self-driving cars due of the potential for technological failure, I share them with you. Just this past month, a self-driving car being tested in Arizona turned a corner, struck, and killed a woman who was crossing the street outside of a crosswalk. We have not perfected this technology. We have miles to go before it can be implemented on any kind of large scale. We have many ethical, legal, and policy considerations and implications to work through. However, we have the opportunity to save countless lives. We have the opportunity to save countless others from debilitating injury. We have the obligation to use technology the way it’s always been used—to benefit our society and improve the lives of all Americans.

Every year, 33,000 people die in automobile accidents, and most of these deaths could have been prevented because the drivers were distracted, drunk, tired, or otherwise should not have been driving.  In their article, "Top 20 Pros and Cons Associated with Self-Driving Cars," the Auto Insurance Center reports that 81% of automobile accidents are due to human error. Reducing the number of vehicle accidents by 10% would be a huge win. Reducing it by 80% would be astounding. A progression from tens of thousands of traffic fatalities annually due to human error to hypothetically minuscule traffic fatalities due to technological errors alone makes self-driving cars extremely beneficial to society. 
	If that statistic is not sobering enough, consider this. Vehicular accidents are the number one cause of death for teenagers, and again, the majority of these fatalities are preventable. We know from cognitive scientists and psychologists that teenagers are physiologically more likely to engage in risky behavior—such as dangerous, drunk, or otherwise impaired driving— which leads teenagers to take driving risks that adults do not. Unfortunately, we read stories and see horrific pictures in this and other newspapers across the country of teens who have been tragically killed, paralyzed or seriously injured due to car accidents. Each year, high schools around the country ask students to sign the Prom Promise, which is a petition that encourages students not to drink and drive on Prom night. Turning sixteen and getting a driver’s license has long been a rite-of-passage in America. The freedom teens feel when they buy their first car and are able to just go is powerful. Self-driving cars would not strip this freedom and independence from teens--a driver's license and could easily be replaced by a self-driving car operator license--but the self-driving cars would save countless lives while allowing teens their beloved independence and their parents peace of mind. 
	If you have reservations about self-driving cars due of the potential for technological failure, I share them with you. Just this past month, a self-driving car being tested in Arizona turned a corner, struck, and killed a woman who was crossing the street outside of a crosswalk. We have not perfected this technology. We have miles to go before it can be implemented on any kind of large scale. We have many ethical, legal, and policy considerations and implications to work through. However, we have the opportunity to save countless lives. We have the opportunity to save countless others from debilitating injury. We have the obligation to use technology the way it’s always been used—to benefit our society and improve the lives of all Americans.

output_text:
There are strong reasons for the United States to transition to self-driving cars. The most compelling is the potential to save lives by removing intoxicated, distracted, or otherwise unsafe human drivers from the road. Autonomous systems can provide a more consistent, precise alternative to the predictable errors people make each time they drive. In addition, autonomous vehicles promise reductions in insurance costs and the need for some outdated infrastructure, improved traffic flow, and environmental benefits through more efficient driving and fleet electrification. Yet the transition carries challenges: a difficult adjustment period, high upfront costs, technological failures, and job displacement in driving-related industries.

When weighing arguments about technological change, it helps to clarify what we mean by “society” and “the public good.” Who benefits? What should society prioritize? How much burden should individuals bear for collective gains? In this case the answers are straightforward. Technologies that have the potential to prevent death and serious injury serve both individual and public interests. Even given current limitations — imperfect sensing and decision-making, transitional costs, and legal and ethical questions — the promise of saving lives makes autonomous vehicles worth pursuing. No other consideration should eclipse that core moral imperative.

America has a persistent, deadly driving problem. Roughly 33,000 people die in automobile accidents annually, and a large share of those fatalities are attributable to human causes: distraction, impairment, fatigue, or poor judgment. Vehicular crashes are also the leading cause of death for teenagers, a demographic particularly prone to risky behavior behind the wheel. When acquiring a driver’s license continues to be treated as a cultural rite of passage, we must not ignore the senseless toll that preventable accidents exact.

The value of preventing these fatalities goes beyond statistics. While regulatory frameworks sometimes assign a monetary figure to a statistical life — for example, the Department of Transportation has used estimates in the millions — a human life is irreplaceable. No model or price tag captures the loss experienced by families and communities.

Concerns about autonomous vehicles are legitimate. Fatal incidents during testing, such as the recent case in Arizona where a self-driving vehicle struck a pedestrian, underscore that the technology is not yet flawless. There remains substantial work to refine sensors, software, and safety protocols, and to resolve ethical, legal, and policy questions before widescale deployment. Still, these challenges are reasons to proceed carefully, not to abandon the project. The potential to prevent countless deaths and severe injuries creates an obligation to develop and regulate autonomous systems responsibly.

In short, self-driving cars are not a panacea, but they represent a significant opportunity to reduce preventable harm on our roads. Despite the obstacles and transitional costs, the moral case for pursuing technology that can save lives is clear: developing autonomous vehicles should be a priority for improving public safety and the well-being of Americans.

output_text:
When Back to the Future I and II premiered in the 1980s, audiences were enthralled by the futuristic technologies on screen. Although technology has advanced rapidly since then, we still don’t have flying cars or hovering skateboards. As we approached 2020, however, some once-fanciful ideas—like autonomous vehicles—began to feel attainable. Olivier Garret wrote in Forbes that he expected 10 million self-driving cars on the road by 2020 and projected that one in four cars would be autonomous by 2030. While Back to the Future II predicted flying cars by 2015, Garret’s forecast for self-driving cars was not far off.

In the past few years, companies such as Ford, Honda, BMW and Tesla have been among the automakers aggressively developing self-driving technology. This push makes sense: technology advances continually as long as demand exists. Because autonomous vehicles are still relatively new and large-scale research is ongoing, it’s important to weigh their advantages and disadvantages before widespread adoption.

Key benefits of self-driving cars include reduced human error, since machines follow traffic laws consistently; fewer distracted drivers, freeing people to multitask safely; and improved transportation efficiency overall. As a proponent of innovation, I believe these advantages outweigh the drawbacks. Automotive technology is progressing rapidly, and vehicles must evolve to keep pace. Still, public hesitation is understandable, because several serious concerns deserve attention before implementation.

Certain jobs would be affected: driver education instructors, DMV clerks and drivers for ride-hailing services like Uber and Lyft could see reduced demand as autonomous fleets expand, potentially increasing unemployment in those sectors. Equally worrisome is technology failure—software glitches or sensor malfunctions could lead to accidents that might be difficult to anticipate. That, in turn, could drive up insurance premiums. Insurers might introduce new coverage categories tailored to autonomous systems, expanding the range of policies and fees available. Given how costly auto insurance already is, additional monthly charges would be a real concern for consumers.

Overall, self-driving cars are entering society rapidly, bringing both promising benefits and significant challenges. More research, regulation and testing are needed to ensure safety, fairness and economic transition plans for displaced workers. Personally, as someone who dislikes driving and fears accidents, I would prefer an autonomous vehicle to a conventional car—provided the technology proves reliable and the risks are responsibly managed.

The world is on the brink of environmental disaster. Irreparable damage has already be done, but further harm can be minimized through the advancement of many technologies, self-driving cars are one. Beyond the greater fuel efficiency self-driving cars provide, there is one feature that people do not often consider. Self-driving cars do not need to be owned by an individual. They can be owned by company, such as Zip Car or Uber. Consumers can pay these companies a monthly fee to have a car available to them whenever they need it and they can also opt in to a car pool so that the car picks up others going to the same place they are. Many people drive a car to work and home again, but they do not use the car throughout the day and they certainly don't use it while they are asleep. During times of non-use, the car could be picking up other riders. This would reduce the number of cars on the road, parked on the sides of streets, and adding to the carbon footprint.

There are valid concerns to this. People worry about the loss of jobs associated with self-driving cars as taxi drivers and truck drivers may become a job of the past. However, the job market is constantly evolving. It was not long ago when cars did not exist, ergo these jobs did not exist. The future will bring new fields for people to work in and new streams of revenue. Change is daunting, and many people are nervous about how a world with self-driving cars will function, but there is no question that it will function. Self-driving cars have arrived and are on the market even if their drivers wouldn't define them as such. Drivers are using cars that will auto park, brake if they approach an obstruction, and follow a car in front of them. They may not feel like they are self-driving as they do still require some human interaction and drivers can opt-in to such features, but when they are all used in combination, the car is in effect driving itself. Even when not in control of the wheel, these features can alert drivers to cars in their blind spot and provide a rear view when the driver is in moving backwards. Whether or not people want this future is now irrelevant; it's coming and soon people will be so used to it that they won’t remember what it was like to drive without a little backup support. Eventually, they won’t remember what it was like to drive at all, and that’s a good thing. Everyone will be safer when computers take the wheel.
	People are terrible drivers. We as humans like to believe that we can stay awake, pay attention, and maneuver an enormous hunk of metal at high speeds. The reality is that even when focused on the road, the human brain can only absorb a limited amount of its surroundings. People worry that self-driving cars are not as good as humans at detecting pedestrians or other anomalies, and they aren't as good--they're better at it. Computers have the capability to process the entire environment, not just what's in their fovea or what's left over after the brain has filtered out what it considers irrelevant. And they do it without getting drowsy or lost in their thoughts. Self-driving cars have years of clocked hours on the road. In all of those hours, only one pedestrian fatality has been caused by a self-driving car in the United States. On the other hand, 23 cyclists were killed in 2017 by human-driven cars in New York City alone (CBS New York). Clearly self-driving cars already have a better track record than their predecessors. 
	However, people should not be surrendering unmonitored control of the driving to their car just yet. There are some edges to human cognition that self-driving cars do not currently share even if they surpass it on others. One of them is the knowledge that some caution should be taken when accelerating around turns or at the peak of hills. I have been in cars that are set to regulate their speed to conditions of the road and the drivers around them, but on a nice day with no other cars in sight of the cameras, they will ramp up speed as they reach the peak of the hill without any knowledge of what may be on the other side. Due to the superior reaction times of a computer as compared to a human, the car could likely avoid any unforeseen obstruction that was just at the top and out of sight of the hill, but its passengers would not have enjoyed the abrupt stop. Even without an accident, such an abrupt stop can cause air bags to deploy or cause the body to jerk against the seatbelt and snap the collar bone. Such a need for abrupt stops would have been eliminated by a careful human driver who slowed down in anticipation of what might be lurking just beyond the crest of the hill. 
	The fact that self-driving cars are not ready to chauffeur people around en masse yet does not mean that it’s not coming though or that this reality is anything to fear. These cars just need more training, and unlike people, when one car trains, they all learn. Whether its navigating the Pittsburgh left or learning to be cautious when its camera does not detect straight road ahead, when one car learns, they all learn because they all share the technology. Thankfully, for the consumer, the transition to a self-driving car is a slow one. People will have time to adjust to the new reality and the software engineers will use this time to improve the technology. We have moved from basic cruise control to cars that will match pace with traffic. We have moved from rear-view cameras to cars that parallel park themselves. People are feeling safer about letting their car take over and when consumers are ready to give up the wheel, the car manufacturers will be there with a self-driving version. When that day comes, we’ll all be safer for it, and maybe the number of cyclists killed in New York City in a given year will finally reach zero. 
	CBSNewYork. “NYC Pedestrian Deaths Drop 32 Percent Last Year.” CBS New York, CBS New York, 8 Jan. 2018, newyork.cbslocal.com/2018/01/08/2017-pedestrian-deaths-nyc/.

Self-driving cars are already among us. They have been piloted in Silicon Valley, San Francisco, New Mexico, Pittsburgh, Moscow, and elsewhere. Yet despite these demonstrations, autonomous vehicles are not yet an everyday presence. Current systems struggle in adverse weather and with rare or chaotic traffic situations—like a storm that disables traffic lights. Still, the development of their neural networks continues because once the technology is reliable, the benefits will be tangible. Self-driving cars could give people with disabilities independence for running errands without relying on friends or taxis. They could sharply reduce traffic fatalities, particularly those caused by distraction or impairment. While legitimate concerns remain about deploying fully autonomous vehicles today, the promise for the near future is clear: these cars can make travel safer for passengers and reduce environmental impact.

We face a looming environmental crisis. Much damage is already done, but further harm can be reduced by adopting better technologies—autonomous vehicles among them. Beyond potential gains in fuel efficiency, self-driving cars enable new ownership and usage models that many people overlook. They do not need to be individually owned; fleets operated by companies like car-share or ride-hail services can provide on-demand transportation. Users could subscribe to a service that offers a vehicle whenever needed and opt into shared rides that pick up others heading the same way. Many personal cars sit idle for most of the day and certainly overnight. In a fleet model, those idle hours become opportunities to transport others. That reduces the total number of vehicles on the road and parked in public spaces, cutting congestion and lowering collective carbon emissions.

Valid concerns remain. People fear job losses as taxi, delivery, and long-haul driving roles evolve or disappear. Yet labor markets have always changed with technology. A century ago, many of today’s jobs didn’t exist because the automobile itself was new. Technological shifts create different kinds of employment—maintenance and oversight of autonomous fleets, new logistics roles, software and sensor industries, and more. Change is unsettling; many are anxious about how a world of self-driving cars will operate. But history shows people adapt. With thoughtful policy—retraining programs, safety standards, and equitable deployment—autonomous vehicles can be integrated in ways that enhance mobility, safety, and environmental outcomes. The transition will be challenging, but the potential benefits make it a change worth pursuing.

Improvements in road infrastructure have reduced the effort required to drive, and self-driving cars promise to eliminate much of the daily cognitive load associated with operating a vehicle. This technology could especially benefit taxi drivers and others for whom driving is a job rather than a choice. Although I support research in this area, I remain skeptical about widespread implementation of fully autonomous vehicles.

Self-driving cars could substantially reduce the effort required to travel, making mobility easier for some but redundant for others who view driving as a pleasure. Having been raised in the foothills of the Himalayas, I never experienced the best infrastructure or perfectly maintained roads. Winding, hazardous mountain routes demand intense concentration; driving there is a continual challenge and will likely remain so. Autonomous vehicles have been tested and performed well in places like the United States, a country known for extensive infrastructure and a strong automobile culture. However, the U.S. cannot be the sole benchmark for global deployment.

Current self-driving systems depend heavily on accurate mapping and GPS, technologies that are not uniformly reliable everywhere. GPS signals suffer from weather-related degradation and local inaccuracies, and in many remote or rugged regions high-fidelity maps do not exist. Autonomous driving also relies on machine learning, and no algorithm achieves perfect accuracy. These limitations raise real concerns about unexpected scenarios and system failures.

Another major issue is human readiness to operate or supervise advanced technology. Many people, particularly older adults, are reluctant or nervous about relinquishing control to a machine. Hesitation or misuse of automation can cause confusion or delayed responses in emergencies, potentially making situations more dangerous rather than safer. Even setting aside human factors, the financial cost of deploying autonomous vehicle infrastructure at scale is enormous. Implementing and maintaining high-precision mapping, communication networks, and upgraded roadways may outweigh the anticipated benefits in many regions.

The promise of fewer accidents is one of the strongest arguments for self-driving cars, but that promise has faced skepticism. There have already been incidents involving autonomous vehicles, demonstrating that automation does not eliminate risk. A sudden malfunction can be difficult for a human operator to diagnose or correct in time, and loss of manual control in seconds can lead to severe consequences. On narrow, curving Himalayan roads where drivers must focus on each bend, an automated system failure could be particularly dangerous.

In conclusion, self-driving vehicles are a compelling and valuable idea, but their success depends on many constraints: infrastructure quality, GPS and mapping reliability, weather resilience, human factors, and financial feasibility. Widespread implementation is not yet practical in many parts of the world, though autonomous technology can be useful where conditions meet these requirements. Continued research and targeted deployment are the prudent paths forward.

output_text:
Self-driving vehicles promise to reduce fatalities, shorten commutes, cut public-transit expenses, and expand mobility options for people with disabilities. Transportation touches nearly every member of a community; many everyday problems trace back to travel — waking earlier to make up time lost in traffic, or receiving a call that a loved one was in an accident. Widespread adoption of autonomous vehicles will positively affect daily life for both owners and nonowners.

The U.S. Department of Transportation currently places the statistical value of a life at about $9.2 million. Safer roads facilitated by autonomous driving — primarily through the reduction of human error — could prevent many crashes and their associated costs. As accidents decline, insurers will face fewer claims and pressure to lower premiums will increase to stay competitive. Greater production scale and improved manufacturing and distribution methods will also reduce vehicle costs. Those savings can extend to local and state governments: with fewer riders and greater flexibility in mobility, some mass-transit systems can be restructured, and maintenance costs for buses and trains may fall. The resulting budget relief could benefit taxpayers and enable reinvestment in targeted transit or infrastructure projects.

A notable caveat is the impact on employment. Reduced demand for public transit and traditional taxi services may displace many workers. However, new opportunities will emerge: demand for engineers, autonomous-vehicle technicians, software developers, vehicle maintenance specialists, and people to manage and repair roadways will grow. Economies will shift, but history suggests technological transitions create different kinds of jobs as older ones decline.

Both proponents and opponents of autonomous vehicles raise legitimate concerns. Yet if society assigns a monetary value to human life, it becomes logical to pursue technologies that reduce fatalities. Continued development of safety systems in autonomous vehicles — coupled with increasing adoption — should drive down incidents of drunk, drowsy, and distracted driving. Over time, these improvements can make roads measurably safer for everyone.

The article "Top 20 Pros and Cons Associated With Self-Driving Cars", provides an in depth look into the reasoning of whether or not we, as a society, should adopt self-driving vehicles.  Of the 20 "pros" listed, an increase in safety was likely the leading factor, but also included were the benefits in commute times and city travel, the possibility for the driver to do something productive in the time that would otherwise be used for driving, and the idea that elderly or disabled people would be able to ride self-driving cars without worry.  However, in contrast to the advantages listed in the article, the author also offered 20 disadvantages.  Many of these focused on the training involved in learning how to operate it, the high cost, security issues (both personal and cybersecurity), legal ramifications, the fact that a self-driving vehicle will not be able to appropriately respond to all situations and other technical hangups,and the financial implications caused by uprooting an old industry. 

When weighing the "pros" and "cons" in order to gauge whether adopting self-driving cars would be beneficial to society, it is important to look at the whole picture.  The "pros" are all valid, while it appears that many of the "cons" can be addressed as the self-driving vehicles further develop.  For example, the high initial cost is cited as a reason against adopting these vehicles.  However, what isn't taken into consideration in this case is the fact that new technologies typically drastically drop in price over a short amount of time.  We often hear the story of the first computers being the size of an entire room and costing hundreds of thousands of dollars at the time.  However, in a few tens of years the price of those computers came down enough that many families could afford them.  I believe a similar situation could occur with the sensor and computer package required in self-driving vehicles.  As a second example, cybersecurity has become a very hot topic recently and appears in the "cons" against these vehicles.  However, several very critical pieces of infrastructure are operated and they have been largely protected against cybersecurity attacks.  Take for example remote controlled drones currently in use by the military.  The risk of a cyber attack is definitely present, but the designers have found a way to adequately protect the devices.  Something similar could be done for self-driving vehicles.

Although several more examples supporting the idea that many of the present "cons" seen in self-driving vehicle adoption can be corrected in the future, it is unnecessary seeing that they are quite similar.  However, what is important to notice is that all of the "pros" do not seem as if they will be changing, even as the vehicles themselves improve.  Therefore I recommend the gradual adoption of self-driving vehicles into society.

output_text:
The article "Top 20 Pros and Cons Associated With Self-Driving Cars" examines whether society should embrace autonomous vehicles. Among the 20 pros listed, increased safety stands out as the primary argument. Other benefits include reduced commute times and smoother city travel, the opportunity for occupants to use travel time productively, and expanded mobility for elderly or disabled individuals who might otherwise be unable to drive. 

Opposing these advantages, the article presents 20 cons. Many concern the training required to interact with or oversee these systems, high initial costs, and security risks—both to personal safety and from cyberattacks. The author also highlights legal and liability issues, the inability of autonomous systems to handle every possible scenario, technical limitations, and the economic disruption that could result from transforming an established industry.

When weighing these pros and cons to decide whether to adopt self-driving cars, it is important to consider the full context. The pros are compelling and, importantly, appear robust as the technology evolves. Many of the cons, by contrast, seem addressable over time. For instance, the high upfront cost is often cited as a barrier. Yet history shows that new technologies typically fall in price as production scales and as component costs decline. Early computers were once room-sized and prohibitively expensive; decades later, personal computers became affordable for most households. A similar trend could reduce the cost of sensors and computing packages required for autonomous vehicles.

Cybersecurity is another frequent concern. While the risk of hacking is real, critical infrastructures and military systems—such as remotely piloted drones—operate with robust protections in place. Designers of those systems have developed techniques and protocols to mitigate threats. Comparable approaches could be adapted and refined for consumer autonomous vehicles, reducing vulnerability over time.

There are additional cons that merit careful attention, but many are variations on these core themes—cost, security, legal frameworks, and technical maturity—and can be mitigated through engineering advances, regulation, and market adoption. Meanwhile, the core pros, especially improvements in safety and accessibility, are unlikely to vanish as the technology improves.

Given the balance of durable benefits and addressable challenges, a gradual, regulated adoption of self-driving vehicles is prudent. This approach allows society to capture the advantages—safer roads, greater mobility, and more productive travel time—while addressing the technical, legal, and economic issues that accompany such a major shift.

output_text:
While reading the article, several advantages and disadvantages of self-driving cars stood out to me. One of the clearest benefits is the potential to reduce the number of car crashes. The U.S. Department of Transportation assigns a value of roughly $9,000,200 to each human life, so fewer fatalities and serious injuries would translate into major cost savings for insurance and healthcare related to accident recovery. Autonomous vehicles also free drivers to complete other tasks during travel, improve traffic flow, and reduce congestion. As a result, commute times in heavily trafficked areas could decrease and fuel waste could be reduced. Larger cities could provide better transportation options for residents, and some administrative costs—such as those associated with issuing driver's licenses—might decline.

However, there are notable drawbacks. Self-driving cars may be unaffordable for low-income people, and until adoption is widespread, accidents, time losses, and costs will still occur. Widespread adoption could displace many workers in transportation sectors, especially freight and taxi drivers, potentially increasing unemployment and harming the economy. Environmental conditions, such as heavy rain, can damage or reduce the effectiveness of sensors like LiDAR and cameras. There is also the risk that human drivers will lose or fail to maintain the skills needed to operate vehicles safely when manual control is required.

Despite these concerns, I believe self-driving cars are becoming more advantageous than disadvantageous in today’s society. After reading the article, I have a clearer understanding and am convinced of their benefits. Since their introduction, autonomous systems have improved substantially in mechanics, accuracy, and algorithms. A decade ago, in 2008, I might have been undecided about supporting self-driving cars; now the advances are convincing.

Most importantly, autonomous vehicles have the potential to save tens of thousands of lives each year. McKinsey & Company reported in 2015 that research indicated self-driving cars could reduce traffic fatalities by up to 90% once fully deployed. The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration reported more than 37,000 motor vehicle deaths in 2016, making crashes a leading cause of death in the United States. Given this scale, self-driving cars represent one of the best available solutions to decreasing motor vehicle deaths today.

Another advantage is the potential reduction in police interactions related to traffic violations. In some cases, routine traffic stops can escalate into confrontations, excessive force, or arrests for unrelated reasons. If autonomous vehicles reduce the incidence of traffic violations, they may also reduce these dangerous and unjust encounters.

In conclusion, while self-driving cars pose legitimate economic, technical, and social challenges, their potential to save lives, lower costs, improve traffic efficiency, and reduce harmful police encounters makes them a positive development for society. Careful policy, equitable access, and continued technological improvement can help mitigate the disadvantages as adoption grows.

draft1:
The new technology of self-driving cars is decades in the making in Pittsburgh, with Carnegie Mellon first leading the way in the 1990s.  Since then the city has been a literal testing ground for companies like Uber and Argo AI, with the mayor keen on attracting (and keeping) talent at the forefront of this technology.  Reactions have long been mixed, though, even before a fatal accident in Arizona recently halted Uber automated test drives.  The strong opposition to automated vehicles in the aftermath, however, is somewhat surprising, given how much safer the technology tends to be.  Or, rather, how unsafe human drivers actually are.

A single death at the hands of an automated Uber stopped its vehicle testing, yet eighty-one percent of vehicular accidents are the result of human error.  Those in working in Oakland routinely see that this human error extends to pedestrians and cyclists in addition to human drivers.  Humans lack the reaction time to respond to unexpected changes in scenery, from a pedestrian stepping out onto Forbes to a car swerving abruptly to miss a pothole.  Human drivers are also increasingly distracted, and are choosing to distract themselves with a dizzying array of activities from personal grooming to streaming television.  Self-driving cars also solve the problem of drunk driving, as autonomous vehicles double as designated drivers.  In a city with limited taxi service and practically no commercial designated driving companies, drunk drivers find themselves tempted to drive from the Southside to the South Hills with often destructive consequences.

Accessibility and inclusion are on residents' minds, too, as the city plans to implement a rapid transit system that will make current connections faster but will leave lower income and disabled locals out of the discussion.  Fleets of self-driving cars would not only ferry drunk passengers but others without access to vehicles as well, including those in neighborhoods lacking in public transportation.  Pittsburgh's elderly and disabled, who used to be better served by paratransit services before budget cuts gutted these systems, are especially vulnerable by their lack of access.

The counter to the accessibility argument is, of course, the high cost of self-driving vehicles.  With an initial cost of more than $100,000, there is little overlap between the people who currently rely on public transportation and those who can immediately purchase self-driving vehicles.  Not many car owners could afford a $100,000 vehicle, for that matter.  This is alleviated by two considerations, though.  The cost of this technology will inevitably go down, just like the brand new CDs, Nintendos, and personal computers of our youth.  What is unaffordable now may not be in a few years.  What is less certain, though, is whether the city can shed its car-first mentality and embrace shared self-driving vehicles, a public personal transit system.

Humans lack the reaction time to respond to unexpected changes in scenery, from a pedestrian stepping out onto Forbes to a car swerving abruptly to miss a pothole. Human drivers are also increasingly distracted, and are choosing to distract themselves with an obscene array of activities from personal grooming to streaming television. For those distracted and inhibited by alcohol, self-driving cars also solve the problem of drunk driving, as autonomous vehicles double as designated drivers. In a city with limited taxi service and practically no private designated driving companies, drunk drivers find themselves tempted to make the drive from the Southside to the South Hills, with often destructive consequences. 
	Accessibility and inclusion are on residents' minds, too, as the city plans to implement a rapid transit system that will make current connections faster but will leave lower-income and disabled locals on the curb. Fleets of self-driving cars would not only ferry drunk passengers but others without access to vehicles as well, including those in neighborhoods lacking in public transportation. Pittsburgh's elderly and disabled, who used to be better served by paratransit services before budget cuts gutted these systems, are especially vulnerable by their lack of access. 
	The counter to the accessibility argument is, of course, the high cost of self-driving vehicles. With an initial outlay of more than $100,000, there is little overlap between the people who currently rely on public transportation and those who can immediately purchase self-driving vehicles. Not many car owners could afford a $100,000 vehicle, for that matter. This is alleviated by two considerations, though. The cost of this technology will inevitably go down, just like the brand new CDs, Nintendos, and personal computers of our youth. What is unaffordable now may not be in a few years. What is less certain, though, is whether the city can shed its car-first mentality and embrace shared self-driving vehicles, a public personal transit system.  Uber and Lyft have revolutionized Pittsburgh's taxi scene in just a few years, after all, and those improvements to accessibility and availability will improve as the numbers of self-driving cars increase.
	Few weeks go by where out city is not making national headlines for its livability, its technology, and its overall rust-belt renaissance. Self-driving cars are currently at the vanguard of this movement, and both attract talent to the region and retain the minds trained by Carnegie Mellon and Pitt. While this has a cachet of modernity and elitism, the benefits of self-driving cars reach everyone. Indeed, pockets of our city now under served and under represented will better enjoy the benefits of our "rust belt renaissance."  When people really look at the costs of human drivers---and human errors---the choice to adapt self-driving cars throughout the city will be an easy one.

Since then the city has served as a testing ground for companies such as Uber and Argo AI, and local government has actively courted the talent and investment that accompany this work. Public reaction has long been mixed, and resistance intensified after a recent fatal crash involving an automated Uber in Arizona. That outcry is striking, however, given how often human error — not technology — is to blame for crashes.

One automated-test fatality halted Uber’s trials, yet studies show roughly eighty-one percent of vehicle accidents stem from human mistakes. In Pittsburgh’s Oakland neighborhood and elsewhere, observers see the consequences of those mistakes affecting not only drivers but also pedestrians and cyclists. Distraction compounds the problem: drivers increasingly divide attention among tasks from grooming to streaming video. Pittsburgh’s elderly and disabled populations, once better served by paratransit before budget cuts reduced those services, are particularly vulnerable to gaps in transportation access.

Another major advantage will be their service to the disabled and elderly people. Once a good proportion of cars on the street become self-drivers, the advocates claim that the whole society will benefit: saving lives, avoiding financial setbacks from car accidents, less fuel consumption, fewer traffic police officers on duty, etc. 

However, the opponents of the technology have some legitimate concerns. In this category there are some superficial and trivial arguments that can be refuted right away. For example, the phobia of a scenario where a car breaks down and the human driver, having forgotten how to drive, has no idea of what to do, leading to chaos in the city cannot be a real argument against self-driving cars. Already today, an average individual has no skill to get a car to start up if it all of a sudden stops in the middle of street. Such a situation rarely disrupts a whole city! Some other arguments question the limits of the technology. What if it snows? What if the road is full of potholes (such as those in Pittsburgh!)? Well, these while valid concerns do not prove the technology insufficient. There are improvements coming every day and all those conditions will be taken care of.

With the obvious criticism of the self-driving cars out of way, we arrive at some deep issues against the technology. On the infrastructure level, there is the problem of providing cyber security to such a delicate network of interconnected software that store and communicate data between cars. If, as Guardian suggests, there is a potential for hackers to manipulate such a system, then it will be a true disaster. Achieving a high level of security while possible could be prohibitively costly, overshadowing any amount of saving on gasoline.

However, the biggest challenge facing self-driving cars is their popularity. The pros listed above all are predicated on the hope that they will come in large quantities. The effect of having cars coordinate their paths will not be felt if they substitute only 2% of all the cars out there. Same holds true for safety. Have you noticed that sometimes, ironically, the safest drivers, are the very cause of some accidents? Imagine a road where the speed limit is 50, but "everyone knows that it is ok to go 60 (when the police is not around!)". Then that one guy who sticks to that 50 mph limit is the source of frustration for those behind him/her and a possible danger for everyone involved. Thus, the advantages of autonomous cars will begin to take effect only after a good portion of the society adopts them.

But with the currently high cost of these cars, and the natural public resistance to change, it seems that self-driving cars will, for the coming years, live in the margins, only triggering imagination and debates from time to time. At this point in time, we are just not ready yet to embrace them -- definitely not in large numbers.

output_text:
It may seem that cars have always been the same: four wheels, a body, a steering wheel. Compared with the history of bicycles, where early black-and-white photos reveal a lengthy, almost biological evolution, the automobile appears deceptively simple. Yet something on the horizon promises to upend much about how we use cars (though, practically speaking, they will probably still sit on four wheels).

Self-driving cars are already here and driving a heated debate about their benefits and risks. Supporters argue that autonomous vehicles could eliminate a large share of fatal crashes because machines don’t get distracted by music, pedestrians, or alcohol and drugs. Another frequently cited advantage is vehicle-to-vehicle and vehicle-to-infrastructure communication: coordinated speeds and signal timing could maximize traffic flow and reduce travel times. Advocates say automated cars will be able to travel closer together thanks to precise control, easing congestion rather than worsening it. A major social benefit would be increased mobility for the elderly and disabled. If a significant portion of vehicles becomes autonomous, proponents claim society would gain in many ways: fewer lives lost, lower economic costs from collisions, reduced fuel consumption, and less need for traffic enforcement.

Opponents raise several legitimate concerns. Some objections are superficial and easily dismissed. The fear that drivers will forget how to operate a vehicle and cause chaos if an autonomous car fails is not new; people already rarely know how to restart a car that stalls, and such incidents seldom disrupt an entire city. Other worries question technological limits: what happens in heavy snow or on roads riddled with potholes? Those are valid points, but they don’t prove the concept unworkable; engineers continually improve sensors and algorithms to handle adverse conditions, and these challenges can be addressed over time.

Beyond these criticisms lie deeper issues. On the infrastructure side, cybersecurity is a major problem. Interconnected software systems that store and share data among cars create attractive targets for hackers. If, as some reports warn, attackers could manipulate networked vehicles, the results could be catastrophic. Implementing robust security is technically feasible but may be expensive enough to offset some of the anticipated savings on fuel and accidents.

Perhaps the most significant hurdle is achieving sufficient market penetration. The touted benefits of coordinated driving and improved safety depend on scale: a fleet of self-driving cars must reach a critical mass before those systemic advantages appear. If autonomous vehicles make up only 2% of the cars on the road, their ability to smooth traffic or prevent collisions will be limited. Moreover, behavioral dynamics matter: sometimes the safest drivers inadvertently cause problems by strictly following rules while others expect informal norms—such as everyone speeding a little—to persist. An autonomous vehicle that rigidly obeys speed limits could become an unexpected hazard in a traffic stream conditioned by different practices. Only when a substantial share of vehicles behaves predictably and cooperatively will many of the promised benefits materialize.

Cost and public resistance to change further slow adoption. Self-driving technology is expensive today, and many people are reluctant to cede control to machines. As a result, for the foreseeable future autonomous cars may remain at the margins of transportation—provoking debate and imagination more than transforming everyday travel.

We are not yet ready to embrace self-driving cars on a large scale. The technology shows great promise, and many obstacles can be overcome with time and investment, but significant technical, economic, and social barriers remain before autonomous vehicles can deliver the sweeping improvements their advocates envision.

output_text:
Self-driving vehicles offer a mix of advantages and disadvantages. One of the most significant benefits, in my view, is the way this technology expands access to automobile transportation for people who otherwise cannot drive. That includes individuals with physical, mental, or emotional disabilities as well as many elderly people. As populations age, giving those who can no longer safely operate a vehicle the ability to travel independently would be a major social gain.

Artificial intelligence can also improve safety by detecting risky situations and responding faster than a human might. Self-driving systems monitor the environment continuously and can react to sudden hazards—objects entering the vehicle’s path, erratic behavior from other drivers, or pedestrians stepping into the road. Features such as autonomous emergency braking demonstrate how a computer can make split-second decisions to stop a car and avoid a collision.

Another clear advantage is parking. In many urban areas parking spaces are shrinking and becoming scarcer, and the stress of finding and maneuvering into tight spots is a common annoyance. A self-parking system can relieve that anxiety by handling the difficult task precisely and consistently. For drivers who dislike or struggle with parking, the ability to sit back and let the vehicle handle it would be a welcome convenience.

Despite these benefits, I remain uneasy about ceding full control of a vehicle to a computer. Computers can malfunction, and when they do, the consequences could endanger the vehicle’s occupants and other road users. A failed system might create hazards that a human driver could have avoided. My distrust is partly cultural: I recall the science-fiction film 2001: A Space Odyssey, in which an onboard computer appears to malfunction, reads the crew’s private conversations, and ultimately acts against them to protect itself. While fictional, the story illustrates fears about autonomous systems behaving in unexpected or adversarial ways.

Beyond malfunctions, there is the human factor: systems can be hacked or manipulated, and malicious actors could turn autonomous vehicles into tools for harm. Terrorism, sophisticated cyberattacks, or even poorly implemented software updates could produce dangerous outcomes.

In sum, self-driving cars hold great promise—greater mobility for many people, enhanced hazard detection, and reduced parking stress—but they also pose real risks from failures, security threats, and unpredictable behavior. Careful engineering, robust cybersecurity, and clear regulatory standards will be essential if society is to reap the benefits while minimizing the dangers.

Self-driving cars appear to be the next wave of technology that will impact our lives, following the pattern previously exploited by the internet, cell phones, and other technological advances. The prospect of cars that autonomously drive is an attractive one for a number of positive reasons. One argument for self-driving cars could be that they would reduce car accidents and therefore save lives. 81 percent of car crashes are the result of human error; therefore, it is appealing to think that computer systems specifically designed to combat the issues associated with human error could be good alternatives. By developing specific software algorithms to respond to a myriad of situations and scenarios, self-driving cars appear to have the capability to handle any situation a human driver might encounter. Because self-driving cars would be fully automated to respond to all situations, it is comforting to think that all actions made in response to situations would lead to the best possible outcomes.
	Unfortunately, we still have a way to go before these prospects and visions about self-driving cars can truly be attained. I would like to argue that we need to spend more time considering the legal and moral dilemmas presented by self-driving cars and their actions, and how these differ from the actions taken by typical human drivers. To exemplify a possible situation in which a legal and moral dilemma might arise, I would like to use a twist on the common ethical conundrum posed by the trolley problem. For our purposes, imagine you are sitting behind the wheel of your car and driving down the street. Seemingly out of nowhere, a ball rolls into the street, followed by a child chasing after it. In the moment in which you see the child, you must make a decision as to how you will react. Your two choices consist of either attempting to brake and hope that you stop in time to not injure the child, or swerving the car to the side of the road and risk hitting a tree, thus injuring yourself. As humans in this type of situation, we would be required to react nearly instantly, therefore it is difficult to fault someone for choosing either of these two options. However, because self-driving cars are programmed in advance, we must as a society decide what the best action would be for the car to take. Would the programmer who decides which action the car should take be held responsible if the car is unable to stop in time to prevent injuring the child? Or would the programmer be held responsible if the car swerves and injures the “driver” after hitting a tree? There does not seem to be an easy answer to these questions, however, if we are going to instruct self-driving cars to act in ways that produce the best outcomes, it is necessary for us to decide what this looks like and how it is manifested in various real-world examples.
	With these questions in mind, it is important that we consider the legal and ethical dilemmas resulting from advances in self-driving car capabilities, before we are ready to implement them throughout our society.
